ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Chinese roasted meat platter rice (Siu Mei San Pin Fan). FOODON_00002809 (cooked meat dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Meat dishes) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for Chinese roasted meat platter rice in FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps specifically to Chinese roasted meat platter rice. SNOMED CT is not used for this record.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 450 g, comprising a bowl of steamed white rice (around 200 g cooked) topped with three portions of roasted meats — typically roasted duck, char siu, and siu yuk — each approximately 80–85 g, plus sauce drizzle.

nutrient_source.title: Siu Mei (Roasted Meat) composite estimate derived from HPB Singapore Foodo database entries: Roasted Duck, Char Siu (Barbecued Pork), Siu Yuk (Roast Pork Belly), and Steamed White Rice
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) — Foodo / Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB entry exists for 'Roasted Platter Rice (Three-Item)' as a composite dish. Values are estimated by weighted combination of HPB Foodo entries for steamed white rice (~44% by weight), roasted duck (~19%), char siu (~19%), and siu yuk (~18%), reflecting a typical 450 g serving composition. Per-100g values are derived from this weighted blend. This is a medium-confidence estimate; exact restaurant formulation at Hennessy Duck - Funan may differ.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed rice-based meals with protein and fat — analogous estimate based on white rice GI modulated by protein and fat co-ingestion; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Plain steamed white rice has a GI of approximately 64–72 (University of Sydney GI Database). However, the substantial protein (~11.8 g/100g) and fat (~8.2 g/100g) content from the roasted meats significantly attenuate the glycemic response of the rice component. A GI of 54 is estimated for this mixed dish, consistent with published evidence that protein- and fat-rich accompaniments reduce the effective GI of white rice meals by 15–25%. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 92.25) / 100 ≈ 49.9, where carbohydrate per 450g serving = 20.5 × 4.5 = 92.25 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg/100g; ~1890 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish, as a single 450 g serving provides approximately 1890 mg sodium — nearly 82% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2300 mg — primarily from soy-based sauces, oyster sauce, and the curing/marinating process used in Siu Mei preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load per serving (~49.9) despite moderate GI (54), driven by large carbohydrate portion from steamed white rice
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should exercise portion control with this dish; while the GI is attenuated to approximately 54 by the protein and fat from roasted meats, the total glycemic load per standard 450 g serving remains high at ~49.9, and reducing the rice portion or substituting with brown rice is recommended to improve postprandial glucose management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from roasted pork belly (siu yuk) and char siu, combined with high sodium and cholesterol content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish infrequently and in reduced portions, as the combination of saturated fat (~2.9 g/100g), cholesterol (~52 mg/100g), and high sodium (~420 mg/100g) from the roasted pork components may adversely affect LDL cholesterol levels and blood pressure; choosing roasted duck over siu yuk and requesting less sauce can partially mitigate cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Roasted Platter Rice (Three-Item), known in Chinese as 三拼饭 (San Pin Fan), is a popular Singaporean Chinese rice dish from the Siu Mei (烧味) roasted meat tradition of Cantonese origin. The dish consists of a generous serving of steamed white jasmine rice topped with three varieties of Chinese roasted meats, most commonly roasted duck (烧鸭), char siu (叉烧, Cantonese barbecued pork glazed with honey and five-spice), and siu yuk (烧肉, crispy-skinned roast pork belly). The meats are pre-roasted in specialist Siu Mei kitchens using traditional methods — duck is roasted whole at high heat to render fat and crisp the skin, char siu is marinated in a sweet-savory sauce and oven-roasted or charcoal-grilled, and siu yuk is slow-roasted with a salt crust to achieve the signature crackling skin. The dish is served with a drizzle of light soy sauce, oyster sauce, and sometimes sesame oil, and may be accompanied by blanched vegetables. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense (~198 kcal/100g) with a moderate protein content (~11.8 g/100g) from the meats, moderate fat (~8.2 g/100g) including saturated fat from pork belly, and a significant carbohydrate load from the rice (~20.5 g/100g). Sodium content is high (~420 mg/100g) due to the sauces and curing processes. The estimated glycemic index is 54 (low), modulated downward from plain white rice by the protein and fat co-ingestion. Clinically, this dish warrants caution for individuals managing hypertension (high sodium load), Type 2 Diabetes (high glycemic load per serving), and cardiovascular disease (saturated fat and cholesterol from pork components). It is a non-halal, non-vegetarian dish containing soy and gluten allergens.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for 'Roasted Platter Rice (Three-Item)' as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted combination of HPB Singapore Foodo component entries (steamed white rice, roasted duck, char siu, siu yuk). GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database white rice values modulated by protein/fat co-ingestion evidence from peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is medium rather than low because all component sources are primary approved sources and the estimation methodology is documented and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are a weighted composite estimate from HPB Foodo component entries; exact values will vary by restaurant and portion composition. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source entries for these components. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source entries for these components. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source entries for these components. 2. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate; gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule. Glycemic load per serving is high (~49.9) due to the large rice portion in a standard 450 g serving. 3. This dish is not halal as it contains pork (char siu and siu yuk). 4. The restaurant Hennessy Duck - Funan (field survey data) serves this dish; exact macronutrient values may differ from this estimate. 5. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy pork and duck fat profiles as HPB Foodo does not always report these sub-fractions; values are approximate. 6. All -1 sentinel values represent nutrients not reported in the primary HPB Foodo source database for these food components.
